original: Psalm 1:3 figures the righteous as a tree planted by streams of water.
english: A life becomes steady when its roots are near what continually feeds it.
spanish: Una vida se vuelve firme cuando sus raíces están cerca de aquello que la alimenta continuamente.
notes: Verified public-source capture. Short interpretive rendering; no long excerpt stored.

original: Psalm 23:2 joins rest, pasture, and still waters.
english: Care is pictured not as command but as being led toward rest.
spanish: El cuidado no aparece como mandato sino como ser conducido hacia el descanso.

original: Psalm 23:4 places fear inside a passage through death-shadow.
english: Courage is not the absence of the valley; it is accompanied passage through it.
spanish: El valor no es ausencia del valle; es paso acompañado a través de él.

original: Psalm 42:7 joins depth, waters, and calling.
english: Depth does not answer surface; it calls to what is already deep enough to hear.
spanish: La profundidad no responde a la superficie; llama a lo que ya es bastante hondo para oír.

original: Psalm 46:10 binds stillness to knowledge of the divine.
english: Knowledge begins where agitation stops performing itself.
spanish: El conocimiento empieza donde la agitación deja de representarse a sí misma.

original: Psalm 51:10 asks for a clean heart and renewed spirit.
english: Transformation is imagined as an inner organ remade, not merely an act forgiven.
spanish: La transformación se imagina como un órgano interior rehecho, no sólo como un acto perdonado.

original: Psalm 90:12 turns mortality into a request for wisdom.
english: To count time rightly is to let finitude teach proportion.
spanish: Contar bien el tiempo es dejar que la finitud enseñe proporción.

original: Psalm 119:105 makes the word a lamp for feet and path.
english: Guidance is not a map of the world; it is enough light for the next step.
spanish: La guía no es un mapa del mundo; es suficiente luz para el siguiente paso.

original: Psalm 130:1 begins prayer from the depths.
english: The cry is not outside the abyss; it begins from within it.
spanish: El clamor no está fuera del abismo; empieza dentro de él.

original: Psalm 137:1 remembers Zion beside the rivers of Babylon.
english: Exile turns landscape into memory's instrument.
spanish: El exilio convierte el paisaje en instrumento de la memoria.

original: Rigveda 10.129 asks about what preceded being and non-being.
english: Cosmogony becomes philosophy when it doubts even the first distinction.
spanish: La cosmogonía se vuelve filosofía cuando duda incluso de la primera distinción.
notes: Verified public-source capture; hymn locator supplied.

original: Rigveda 1.164 includes the formula of one reality named in many ways.
english: Multiplicity of names does not require multiplicity of source.
spanish: La multiplicidad de nombres no exige multiplicidad de fuente.

original: Rigveda 10.90 presents Purusha as a cosmic body from which orders unfold.
english: The world is imagined as dismemberment: order born from a body too vast to remain whole.
spanish: El mundo se imagina como desmembramiento: orden nacido de un cuerpo demasiado vasto para permanecer entero.

original: Rigveda 10.125 personifies speech as a cosmic power moving among gods and beings.
english: Speech is not just expression; it is a power that arranges worlds.
spanish: La palabra no es sólo expresión; es una potencia que ordena mundos.

original: Rigveda 3.62 contains the Gayatri invocation to Savitr's radiance.
english: Illumination is requested as an ordering of intelligence, not only as light seen by the eye.
spanish: La iluminación se pide como ordenamiento de la inteligencia, no sólo como luz vista por el ojo.

original: Rigvedic hymns to Ushas figure dawn as arrival, awakening, and disclosure.
english: Dawn is not merely morning; it is the world's act of becoming available again.
spanish: El alba no es sólo mañana; es el acto por el cual el mundo vuelve a estar disponible.
notes: Verified public-source capture; grouped hymn motif.

original: Mundaka Upanishad gives the image of two birds on one tree.
english: One self eats the fruit; the other watches from the same branch.
spanish: Un yo come el fruto; el otro observa desde la misma rama.

artifact: Katha Upanishad — Chariot of the Self
original: Katha Upanishad uses the chariot as an image of body, senses, mind, and self.
english: The person is a moving assembly: driver, reins, horses, road, and witness.
spanish: La persona es un ensamblaje en movimiento: conductor, riendas, caballos, camino y testigo.

original: Isha Upanishad tradition frames fullness as remaining full even after fullness comes forth.
english: The source does not diminish by overflowing into what it makes.
spanish: La fuente no disminuye al desbordarse en aquello que produce.

original: Chandogya Upanishad repeats the mahāvākya identifying the self with that reality.
english: Instruction culminates when the distant principle is recognized as intimate identity.
spanish: La enseñanza culmina cuando el principio distante se reconoce como identidad íntima.

original: Brihadaranyaka Upanishad uses negation to approach the real beyond predicates.
english: What cannot be held by description is approached by disciplined refusal.
spanish: Lo que no puede sostenerse con descripción se aproxima mediante una negación disciplinada.

original: Chandogya Upanishad uses dissolved salt as an image of unseen pervasiveness.
english: The real may vanish from sight while becoming present everywhere in taste.
spanish: Lo real puede desaparecer de la vista mientras se vuelve presente en todas partes como sabor.

original: Masnavi tells of people touching different parts of an elephant in darkness.
english: Partial contact becomes false certainty when the lamp is missing.
spanish: El contacto parcial se vuelve falsa certeza cuando falta la lámpara.
recommendedEditionSource: E. H. Whinfield — Masnavi i Ma'navi

original: Masnavi tells of a grammarian whose knowledge fails before the river.
english: A theory of swimming cannot save the one who never entered the water.
spanish: Una teoría de la natación no salva a quien nunca entró al agua.
recommendedEditionSource: E. H. Whinfield — Masnavi i Ma'navi

original: Masnavi opens with the reed lamenting separation from its source.
english: Song begins where a cut remembers the reed-bed.
spanish: El canto empieza donde un corte recuerda el cañaveral.
recommendedEditionSource: E. H. Whinfield — Masnavi i Ma'navi

original: Masnavi's Moses and shepherd story tests purity of speech against sincerity of love.
english: Devotion may speak badly and still arrive more truly than correct language without fire.
spanish: La devoción puede hablar mal y aun así llegar con más verdad que el lenguaje correcto sin fuego.
recommendedEditionSource: E. H. Whinfield — Masnavi i Ma'navi

original: Masnavi contrasts painting with polishing in the tale of Chinese and Greek artists.
english: The mirror wins by removing itself from the image.
spanish: El espejo vence retirándose de la imagen.
recommendedEditionSource: E. H. Whinfield — Masnavi i Ma'navi

original: 古池や 蛙飛びこむ 水の音
english: The old pond is not silent after the frog; it becomes audible as a world.
spanish: El viejo estanque no queda silencioso tras la rana; se vuelve audible como mundo.
recommendedEditionSource: The Haiku Foundation — Matsuo Bashō reference
notes: Verified public-source capture; original Japanese retained.

original: 露の世は 露の世ながら さりながら
english: Even knowing the world is dew does not cancel grief for the dewdrop.
spanish: Saber que el mundo es rocío no cancela el duelo por la gota.
recommendedEditionSource: The Haiku Foundation — Kobayashi Issa reference
notes: Verified public-source capture; original Japanese retained.

original: 春の海 終日のたり のたりかな
english: The spring sea teaches motion without urgency.
spanish: El mar de primavera enseña movimiento sin urgencia.
recommendedEditionSource: The Haiku Foundation — Yosa Buson reference
notes: Verified public-source capture; original Japanese retained.

original: Chiyo-ni's morning glory haiku centers a flower, a well bucket, and borrowed water.
english: Attention refuses to break the flower and turns need into borrowing.
spanish: La atención se niega a romper la flor y convierte la necesidad en préstamo.
recommendedEditionSource: The Haiku Foundation — Fukuda Chiyo-ni reference

original: Bashō's cicada haiku lets sound pierce into stone.
english: The smallest sound becomes geological when attention gives it depth.
spanish: El sonido más pequeño se vuelve geológico cuando la atención le da profundidad.
recommendedEditionSource: The Haiku Foundation — Matsuo Bashō reference

artifact: Liber AL — Law as Whole-Life Formula
original: Liber AL I:40 presents the central law as a formula of will.
english: A law becomes esoteric when it names the whole motion of a life, not a rule outside it.
spanish: Una ley se vuelve esotérica cuando nombra el movimiento entero de una vida, no una regla externa.
recommendedEditionSource: O.T.O. public library — Liber AL vel Legis, I:40
notes: Verified public-source capture; verse locator supplied.

original: Liber AL I:3 figures each person as a star.
english: The self is not a unit in a crowd; it is an orbiting center with its own law of motion.
spanish: El yo no es una unidad en la multitud; es un centro orbital con su propia ley de movimiento.
recommendedEditionSource: O.T.O. public library — Liber AL vel Legis, I:3

original: Liber AL I:57 binds love to will as formula.
english: Love is not sentiment here; it is force shaped by direction.
spanish: El amor no es sentimentalismo aquí; es fuerza formada por dirección.
recommendedEditionSource: O.T.O. public library — Liber AL vel Legis, I:57

artifact: Liber AL — Nuit as Infinite Space
original: Liber AL I:22 links dividedness, love, and the chance of union.
english: Multiplicity is not a fall from unity; it is the condition that lets union become event.
spanish: La multiplicidad no es caída desde la unidad; es la condición que permite que la unión acontezca.
recommendedEditionSource: O.T.O. public library — Liber AL vel Legis, I:22

artifact: Liber AL — Thelema as Word of Law
original: Liber AL I:39 gives Thelema as the word of the law.
english: A single word condenses doctrine into a seed of practice.
spanish: Una sola palabra condensa la doctrina en semilla de práctica.
recommendedEditionSource: O.T.O. public library — Liber AL vel Legis, I:39

artifact: Liber AL — No Law Beyond Will
original: Liber AL III:60 closes law back into will.
english: The final boundary is not prohibition but the discovery of one's true trajectory.
spanish: El límite final no es la prohibición sino el descubrimiento de la propia trayectoria verdadera.
recommendedEditionSource: O.T.O. public library — Liber AL vel Legis, III:60

original: Desert tradition remembers Arsenius withdrawing into silence and stillness.
english: Silence is not absence of speech; it is a chosen climate for attention.
spanish: El silencio no es ausencia de habla; es un clima elegido para la atención.
recommendedEditionSource: Benedicta Ward — Sayings of the Desert Fathers / public alphabetical witness
notes: Verified source-family capture; source already extensively represented.

artifact: Abba Moses — Sit in Your Cell
original: Desert tradition gives the cell as a place that teaches the monk everything.
english: The room becomes teacher when escape stops interrupting the lesson.
spanish: La habitación se vuelve maestra cuando la fuga deja de interrumpir la lección.
recommendedEditionSource: Benedicta Ward — Sayings of the Desert Fathers / public alphabetical witness

original: Desert sayings repeatedly turn judgment away from others toward one's own work.
english: The discipline begins when blame loses its outward appetite.
spanish: La disciplina empieza cuando la culpa pierde su apetito hacia afuera.
recommendedEditionSource: Benedicta Ward — Sayings of the Desert Fathers / public alphabetical witness

original: Dao De Jing 40 names returning as the movement of the Dao.
english: The path advances by turning back toward what precedes force.
spanish: El camino avanza al volver hacia aquello que precede a la fuerza.
recommendedEditionSource: Chinese Text Project — Dao De Jing 40

artifact: Dao De Jing 78 — Water and Softness
original: Dao De Jing 78 uses water to think weakness overcoming hardness.
english: Softness wins not by striking harder but by refusing the form hardness expects.
spanish: Lo blando vence no golpeando más fuerte sino negándose a la forma que lo duro espera.
recommendedEditionSource: Chinese Text Project — Dao De Jing 78

original: Dao De Jing uses the uncarved block as a figure for unforced simplicity.
english: Before refinement becomes display, simplicity still contains unused worlds.
spanish: Antes de que el refinamiento se vuelva exhibición, la simplicidad aún contiene mundos no usados.
recommendedEditionSource: Chinese Text Project — Dao De Jing

original: Popol Wuuj opens in a world before dawn and before formed human speech.
english: Creation begins as a listening darkness before names settle into things.
spanish: La creación empieza como una oscuridad que escucha antes de que los nombres se fijen en cosas.
recommendedEditionSource: Poopol Wuuj / Popol Vuh, K'ichee'-Spanish witness
notes: Verified digitized-source capture; page locator to refine later.

original: Popol Wuuj names Heart of Sky among the powers of creation.
english: Sky becomes heart when origin is imagined as living center, not empty height.
spanish: El cielo se vuelve corazón cuando el origen se imagina como centro vivo, no como altura vacía.
recommendedEditionSource: Poopol Wuuj / Popol Vuh, K'ichee'-Spanish witness

original: Popol Wuuj forms true humans from corn after failed attempts.
english: The human is not made from abstraction but from food, field, and dependence.
spanish: El humano no está hecho de abstracción sino de alimento, campo y dependencia.
recommendedEditionSource: Poopol Wuuj / Popol Vuh, K'ichee'-Spanish witness

original: Greek Anthology epitaphs often make the tombstone speak for the dead.
english: The stone becomes a mouth because memory needs matter to answer absence.
spanish: La piedra se vuelve boca porque la memoria necesita materia para responder a la ausencia.

original: Greek epitaphs frequently bind burial, sea, travel, and loss.
english: The sea keeps the grave moving after the body has stopped.
spanish: El mar mantiene la tumba en movimiento después de que el cuerpo se ha detenido.

original: Japanese death poems often let moon, autumn, and vanishing stand in for the dying self.
english: The poem lets the person vanish while the moon keeps receiving the night.
spanish: El poema permite que la persona desaparezca mientras la luna sigue recibiendo la noche.
notes: Verified digitized-source capture; exact poet locator to refine later.

original: Japanese death poems frequently compress death into seasonal weather.
english: The final self is not explained; it is thinned into weather.
spanish: El último yo no se explica; se adelgaza hasta volverse clima.

original: Japanese death poems often turn the last moment into road, journey, or departure.
english: Death appears as a road that remains after the walker has gone.
spanish: La muerte aparece como un camino que permanece después de que el caminante se ha ido.
